coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen and iron co-doped carbon quantum dots/MnO2 nanowire composites for flexible solid-state supercapacitors with high areal capacitance
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Guijun
Park, Soo-Jin
coredata.dc:description:
Herein, the synthesis of MnO2 nanowires decorated with transition metal-doped carbon quantum dots (MCQDs) is described for use as binder-free flexible solid-state supercapacitors. In particular, the water-soluble Fe and N co-doped MCQDs prepared by amidation reaction with ferric ammonium citrate and urea had ultrafine particle morphologies and showed super-hydrophilicity in aqueous solution, which greatly improved the contact probability between the electrode and the electrolyte. In addition, the co-doped MCQDs increased both the electrical conductivity and specific capacitance of the MnO2/carbon-based nanocomposites and limited the collapse and exfoliation of the MnO2 structure during extended cycling. Compared with pristine MnO2 electrode, the MCQDs/MnO2 composite electrode exhibited faster charge-discharge rate and improved cycle stability with increased specific capacitance. The flexible symmetric supercapacitor constructed from the MCQDs/MnO2 composite had a capacitance retention of 85.6 % after 10,000 charge/discharge cycles at a current density of 2 mA cm−2. In addition, the device showed a maximum power density of 4.8 W cm−2 at an energy density of 2.3 mW h cm−2, and a maximum energy density of 11.16 mW h cm−2 at a power density of 311.6 mW cm−2.
Data Availability Statement The data that support the findings of this study are available from the corresponding author upon reasonable request.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Composite electrode
Flexible supercapacitor
Manganese dioxide
Metal carbon quantum dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electrochemical performance of MO-C2//MO-C2 symmetrical supercapacitors

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We thank Wenner-Gren Foundations ( UPD2021-0123 ) for financial support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD patterns of MnO2 nanowires (MO-Pure) and MCQDs/MnO2 composites (MO-C1, MO-C2, MO-C3). (b) FT-IR of MCQDs, MnO2 nanowires and MCQDs/MnO2 (MO-C1, MO-C2, MO-C3). (c) Raman of MCQDs, MnO2 nanowires and MCQDs/MnO2 composites. (d) enlarge Raman diagram of MCQDs.

(a) XPS survey spectra of MnO2 nanowires (MO-Pure) and MO-C2. (b) Mn 2p spectra of MnO2 nanowires (MO-Pure) and MO-C2. (c) O 1 s spectra of MnO2 nanowires (MO-Pure) and MO-C2. (d) C 1 s spectra of MO-C2. (e) N 1 s spectra of MO-C2. (f) Fe 2p spectra of MO-C2.

(a) Nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherm and (b) Pore size distribution of MCQDs, MnO2/C composites (MO-C1, MO-C2, MO-C3); (c) Nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherm and (d) Pore size distribution of MnO2 nanowires (MO-Pure).
SEM images of (a-b) MnO2 nanowires (MO-Pure); (c-d) MO-C1; (e-f) MO-C2; (g-h) MO-C3.
FE-TEM images of (a-b) MCQDs; (c-d) MnO2 nanowires (MO-Pure); (e-f) MO-C2.

CV curves of (a) MnO2 nanowires (MO-Pure), (b) MO-C1, (c) MO-C2 and (d) MO-C3 at different scan rate; GCD curves of (e) MnO2 nanowires (MO-Pure), (f) MO-C1, (g) MO-C2 and (h) MO-C3 at different current densities; (i) Areal capacitance as a function of current density calculated from GCD curves; (j) cycling performance of MnO2 nanowires (MO-Pure) and MO-C2, the insets show the last ten charge–discharge cycles of MO-C2; Nyquist plots of (k) MnO2 nanowires (MO-Pure) and (l) MO-C2.

Electrochemical behaviors of MO-C2// MO-C2 symmetric supercapacitor: (a) CV curves under different voltage window. (b) CV curves at different scan rates. (c) GCD curves at different current densities. (d) cycling performance at 2 mA cm−2 and 3 mA cm−2, respectively. (e) Nyquist plots before and after cycles. (f) Ragone plots compared with data in other literature.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen and iron co-doped carbon quantum dots/MnO2 nanowire composites for flexible solid-state supercapacitors with high areal capacitance

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Chalmers University of Technology, Göteborg SE-412 96, Sweden

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Chalmers University of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Chalmers University of Technology, Göteborg, SE-412 96, Sweden

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Inha University, 100 Inharo, Incheon 22212, South Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Inha University, 100 Inharo, Incheon, 22212, South Korea

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Chalmers University of Technology, Göteborg SE-412 96, Sweden.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Chalmers University of Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, the synthesis of MnO2 nanowires decorated with transition metal-doped carbon quantum dots (MCQDs) is described for use as binder-free flexible solid-state supercapacitors. In particular, the water-soluble Fe and N co-doped MCQDs prepared by amidation reaction with ferric ammonium citrate and urea had ultrafine particle morphologies and showed super-hydrophilicity in aqueous solution, which greatly improved the contact probability between the electrode and the electrolyte. In addition, the co-doped MCQDs increased both the electrical conductivity and specific capacitance of the MnO2/carbon-based nanocomposites and limited the collapse and exfoliation of the MnO2 structure during extended cycling. Compared with pristine MnO2 electrode, the MCQDs/MnO2 composite electrode exhibited faster charge-discharge rate and improved cycle stability with increased specific capacitance. The flexible symmetric supercapacitor constructed from the MCQDs/MnO2 composite had a capacitance retention of 85.6 % after 10,000 charge/discharge cycles at a current density of 2 mA cm−2. In addition, the device showed a maximum power density of 4.8 W cm−2 at an energy density of 2.3 mW h cm−2, and a maximum energy density of 11.16 mW h cm−2 at a power density of 311.6 mW cm−2.
The data that support the findings of this study are available from the corresponding author upon reasonable request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the widespread use of micro-electronic devices, the requirements for energy storage devices are getting higher and higher. In particular, flexible, portable and wearable power storage devices have become a major focus for research. Potential candidates for energy storage devices include batteries (e.g., lithium-ion, sodium-ion, fuel cell, etc.) and supercapacitors (e.g., electrical double layer capacitors (EDLCs) and pseudo-capacitors) [1,2]. These two classes of devices display distinct energy storage characteristics due to the involvement of different energy storage mechanisms. Although a battery can possess a high energy density (∼200 W h kg−1) via redox reactions, the diffusion-limited Faraday electrochemical reactions result in low power density and long charging times of, typically, tens of minutes to several hours. In contrast, EDLCs generally use the adsorption of surface ions rather than redox reactions to store energy with lower density (∼5 W h kg−1) but more rapid charging-discharging (on the order of seconds). On the other hand, pseudo-capacitors are distinct members of the supercapacitor class in that they store charges not only by adsorption of surface ions (the same as EDLCs) but also via redox reactions (similarly to batteries) [3,4]. Therefore, pseudocapacitive materials provide a way to achieve both high energy density and high power density. This class of materials includes primarily metal hydroxides, metal oxides, and conductive polymers, which have various oxidation states and structures.

Among the various metal oxide materials, manganese dioxide (MnO2) shows good capacitance characteristics in neutral electrolytes, and is therefore a promising electrode material with great potential [5]. Two main energy storage mechanisms have been described for MnO2 [6,7]. One is the absorption and desorption of protons or metal cations from the electrolyte onto the electrode surface, as summarized by the equation:
Mn
O
2
+
M
+
+
e
−
↔
Mn
O
2
−
M
+
where M is a metal cation (e.g., Li+, Na+, K+, etc.) or H+.

The other mechanism is the extraction/incorporation of metal cations or H+ into the MnO2 lattice during the redox process:
Mn
O
2
+
M
+
+
e
−
↔ MnOOM

This adsorption and insertion of protons or ions can involve not only the surface, but also the bulk phase of the MnO2. This process is accompanied by changes in the valence state of the manganese and the rapid transfer of charge, thus resulting in excellent surface-phase and bulk-phase pseudo-capacitance. If each manganese atom can participate in the pseudocapacitive redox process with one electron, the theoretical specific capacitance value can reach 1370 F g−1 [8]. However, due to its poor intrinsic conductivity, low ionic diffusion constant, and limited surface area, the specific capacitance of pristine MnO2 is much lower than the theoretical value. The specific capacitance of α-MnO2 reaches 195–275 F g−1, 265–320 F g−1 and 310 F g−1 in 2 M KCl, 0.1 M Na2SO4 and 2 M (NH4)2SO4, respectively [9]. But usually the specific capacitance of γ-MnO2 is in the range of 20–30 F g−1 [10]. The specific capacitance of δ-MnO2 synthesized by different methods can exceed 350 F g−1 [11]. There are usually two effective ways to address these problems, one is to synthesize nanostructured MnO2 with large specific surface area to increase the quantity of active sites [12–14], and the other is to combine MnO2 with conductive materials to increase its conductivity [15–18]. In the latter case, carbon-based materials such as graphene, carbon nanotubes, porous carbon, or activated carbon are generally used to combine with MnO2 to attain high conductivity and low-cost electrode materials with requisite high specific capacitance.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as a zero-dimensional (0-D) materials, consist of dispersed spherical carbon particles with very small size (below 10 nm). They found applications in various areas due to their excellent optical properties, good water solubility, low toxicity, environmental friendliness, and abundant raw material source. In recent years, reports on the application of CQDs in supercapacitors [19–21] and batteries [22–24] have continued to emerge unabated. Compared to other carbon materials, the 0-D nature of CQDs grants them greater flexibility to form structures of various shapes. When combined with metal oxides, they provide more pathways and interfaces for ion transfer and volume changes. Furthermore, they display a maximum specific surface area at a diameter of 1–2 nm, which enables them to fulfill the important electrochemical performance criterion of increasing the contact area between electrodes and electrolytes. Since the charge storage mechanism of supercapacitors is based on the surface (or near surface) reaction of the electrode material, the wettability of the electrode material by the electrolyte is an important factor affecting the electrochemical performance. This property can be enhanced by various functional groups found in CQDs, such as hydroxyl, carboxyl and carbonyl groups. In addition, the conjugated system of CQDs consisting of sp2 hybrid orbitals can accelerate the migration of electrons. Moreover, the easy single- or multi-element doping of CQDs (e.g., N, O, S, P) [20,25–28] allows the synthesis of materials with distinct characteristics, thus providing increased possibilities for the preparation of electrodes for multi-purpose applications. CQDs co-doped with first row transition metals (Mn, Fe, Co, Ni, Cu, Zn) have been reported for energy storage materials [29]. It is shown that co-dopants such as Mn2+, Fe2+ and Co2+ can improve the capacitance of CQDs. The improved capacitance may be due to the introduction of high specific surface area of CQDs, the existence of different reactive functional groups on the surface of CQDs, and the addition of transition metal atoms in the carbon source core of CQDs. Among them, the unpaired electrons in the doped transition metals significantly increase the specific capacity of the CQDs. Until now, there have been some reports on the use of CQDs in supercapacitors, including CQDs/NiCo2O4 composites with various morphologies [30], CQDs/RuO2 networks [31], graphene quantum dot/MnO2 nanosheet composites [32], and N-doped graphene quantum dots/Fe3O4/halloysite nanotube composites [33]. In each of these composites, the synergistic effect of CQDs resulted in enhanced electrochemical performance. However, there have been no reports on the preparation of N, Fe codoped CQDs (MCQDs) combined with transition metal oxides (MnO2) into MCQDs/MnO2 composites for application in supercapacitors.

In the present work, N and Fe co-doped metal carbon quantum dots/MnO2 nanowire (MCQDs/MnO2) composites were grown on nickel foam by a hydrothermal method for use as a binder-free flexible solid-state supercapacitor. The design strategies were motivated as follow: (i) the three-dimensional network structure of the nickel foam substrate allows active materials to be efficiently deposited and facilitates the transfer of ions and electrons; (ii) the binder-free design can avoid the "dead surface" caused by the binder and conductive additives in the traditional electrode, thereby providing more effective charge and mass transfer and improving the conductivity of the electrode. In addition, the design provides a larger interface area and rich active sites, which significantly improves the electrochemical performance of the capacitors; (iii) N, Fe co-doped MCQDs have better performance than undoped CQDs. Nitrogen doping can effectively induce a large number of defects in the carbon microstructure and increase the specific surface area to accept more ions during the electrochemical reaction. Iron doping can be present on the surface of MCQDs in the form of iron oxides to provide enhanced pseudocapacitance. These will significantly improve the electrochemical performance of MCQDs/MnO2 composite electrode; (iv) the heteroatom doping process facilitates the growth of electron-rich regions on the electrode surface, and the synthesized N and Fe co-doped MCQDs not only increase the contact area between the electrode and the electrolyte, but also improve the electrical conductivity of the MCQDs/MnO2 nanowire composites, so that the obtained MCQDs/MnO2 nanowire exhibits good electrochemical performance. Furthermore, the electrochemical performance of flexible solid-state symmetrical supercapacitors assembled using the MCQDs/MnO2 nanowire composites were investigated, which provides a new possibility for the development of flexible supercapacitors.

Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was used to determine the actual content of each component in the MCQDs/MnO2 composite samples, as shown in Fig. S1 (Supporting information). The sample was heated from room temperature to 500 °C at a heating rate of 5 °C min−1 under an atmosphere of air. In this process, the MCQDs burned to produce COx gas, whereas the MnO2 did not react. The results in Fig. S1 indicate weight losses of 0.47, 0.81, 2.87 and 5.42 wt% for the MnO2-Pure, MO-C1, MO-C2 and MO-C3 samples, respectively. When the mass of water lost (0.47 %) from the samples were deducted, the final mass percentages of MCQDs were 0.00, 0.34, 2.40 and 4.95 wt%, respectively.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements were performed to determine the structural properties of the MnO2 nanowires and the various MCQDs/MnO2 composites, as shown in Fig. 1a. The obtained composites with MCQDs contents of 0, 1, 5, or 10 wt% are designated hereafter as MO-Pure, MO-C1, MO-C2 and MO-C3, respectively. Here, the diffraction peak of the MCQDs/MnO2 composites is seen to be consistent with the pure MnO2 sample, thus indicating that the addition of MCQDs had no effect on the crystal structure of MnO2. In detail, the diffraction peaks at 12.7°, 18.1°, 28.8°, 37.5°, 41.2°, 41.9°, 56.9°, 65.1°, 67.6° and 72.7° correspond, respectively, to the (110), (200), (310), (211), (420), (301), (431), (002), (710) and (312) planes of tetragonal α-MnO2 (JCPDS no. 00–044–0141). Since part of the pseudo-capacitance of MnO2 comes from the absorption and desorption of protons or metal cations from the electrolyte onto the electrode surface, a larger tunnel space is desirable. In this respect, the channel structure of α-MnO2 consists of [1 × 1] (hole size 0.189 nm) and [2 × 2] (hole size 0.46 nm) tunnel structures, with the large size of the latter being beneficial for metal cation insertion/extraction.

In addition, Fourier transform-infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy was performed to determine the functional groups present on the surfaces of the MCQDs, the MnO2 nanowires, and the MCQDs/MnO2 composites, as shown in Fig. 1b. Here, the FT-IR spectrum of the MCQDs displays a weak peak at 3173 cm−1, which is attributed to the O-H and N-H stretching vibrations. The peak at 2344 cm−1 is attributed to the CC vibration, and the peak at 1559 cm−1 is assigned to the CC and CN vibrations. In addition, the three peaks in the range of 1000–1400 cm−1 are characteristic of the C-O stretching vibrations, and the three peaks in the range of 650–1000 cm−1 are attributed to the CC-H stretching vibrations. Meanwhile, the FT-IR spectrum of the MnO2 nanowire exhibits a peak at 592 cm−1 due to the Mn-O vibration. As expected, the FT-IR spectra of the various MCQDs/MnO2 composites each exhibit not only a peak due to the presence of the MnO2 nanowire, but also the CN and C-O vibration peaks at 1559 cm−1 and 1386 cm−1, respectively, due to the presence of the MCQDs (the enlarged figure is shown on the right of Fig. 1b). Thus, the MCQDs contain a large number of hydrophilic oxygen-containing functional groups which can greatly enhance the wettability of MnO2 by the electrolyte and, thus, improve the utilization rate of the material.

The Raman spectra of the MCQDs, the MnO2 nanowires, and the MCQDs/MnO2 composites are presented in Fig. 1c. Here, the MnO2 nanowires exhibit a main peak at 640 cm−1 which can be identified as the Mn-O symmetric stretching vibration of the [MnO6] octahedron in [MnO2·H2O] compounds and is also characteristic of the tetragonal MnO2 structure. There is also a second peak at lower frequency (374 cm−1) due to the deformation modes of the Mn–O–Mn chain in the MnO2 octahedral lattice [34,35]. In addition, the Raman spectrum of the MCQDs could be used to evaluate the degree of disorder of the graphite. In the enlarged spectrum obtained using visible light excitation (Fig. 1d), the peaks at ∼1360 and 1560 cm−1 represent the D and G bands, respectively, and the intensity ratio (ID/IG) represents the ratio of sp3/sp2 carbon hybridization. The D band is a disorder-induced band that confirms the existence of disorder at the edges, in the form of sp3 carbons, surface states, or functional groups on the MCQD surface. The broadening of the D peak is due to the increase in the sp3 hybridization content of carbon-carbon bonds, carbon-oxygen bonds, and hydroxyl groups. The G band is due to E2 g phonon vibrations at Γ-point, which arises due to strains produced in the sp2 carbon network. The ID/IG is greater than 1 indicating that the defects are present at the surface of CQDs. Compared to that of the MnO2 nanowires, the Raman spectrum of the MCQDs/MnO2 composites is slightly red-shifted. This indicates that a local surface plasmon resonance effect has occurred due to the enhanced local electric field arising from the use of MCQDs to modify the surface of the MnO2 nanowires [36]. This, in turn, enhanced the conductivity and, hence, the electrochemical performance of the MnO2.

To further analyze the elemental composition and chemical bond states on the surface of the MCQDs, the MnO2 nanowires, and the MO-C2 composite, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed. In Fig. 2a, both the full XPS spectra of the MnO2 nanowires and the MO-C2 composite display typical Mn and O peaks, while the successful modification of the MCQDs on the composite surface is indicated by the appearance of the characteristic Fe peak in the full spectrum of the MO-C2. Meanwhile, in the corresponding high-resolution Mn 2p spectra (Fig. 2b), the two main peaks at 653.9/653.2 eV and 642.2/641.5 eV represent the Mn 2p1/2 and Mn 2p3/2 electronic states with a spin energy separation of 11.7 eV. This suggests that the main oxidation state of Mn is + 4, which is consistent with the difference in peak position of MnO2. Moreover, compared with the MnO2 nanowires, the Mn peak of the MO-C2 composite is shifted by 0.7 eV in the direction of low binding energy, thus indicating that the Mn(IV) on the surface of MnO2 was partially reduced to Mn(III) during the surface modification with MCQDs [37]. In addition, the high-resolution O1s spectrum (Fig. 2c) displays three distinct peaks at around 532.1, 531.2, and 529.8 eV which are assigned to the H-O-H, Mn-O-H, and Mn-O-Mn, respectively. Further, the high-resolution C 1s, N 1s, and Fe 2p spectra of the MO-C2 composite (Fig. 2d-f) demonstrate the coexistence of the CO, C−C, CC, C−N, and C−N+ bonds and the Fe2+/Fe3+ oxidation states (the binding energy of the Fe 2p is shown in Table 1) [38]. The elemental composition of the MO-C2 composite is shown to be 11.9 % Mn, 42.53 % O, 1.87 % N, 41.32 % C, and 2.38 % Fe. Meanwhile, in Fig. S2, the MCQDs are shown to be mainly composed of 47.76 % C, 9.41 % N, 39.76 % O and 3.07 % Fe. The results of the XPS analysis are consistent with those of FT-IR, thus confirming the presence of hydrophilic oxygen-containing functional groups which could improve the wettability between the electrode and the electrolyte and reduce the ionic diffusion resistance of the electrolyte on the electrode surface. As noted above, these effects could improve the capacitance performance and the utilization rate of MnO2 electrode materials.

The wettability of the material is very important because it indicates whether the electrolyte can be easily adsorbed by the material. A surface with a contact angle of less than 90° facilitates the close contact of electrolyte ions with the MnO2. Since the surface free energy is inversely proportional to the contact angle, high surface free energy is beneficial for obtaining more interfaces between the active electrode material and the electrolyte. To explore the effect of the MCQDs treatment on the hydrophilicity of MnO2, a static contact angle instrument was used to measure the contact angle between a droplet of water and the surfaces of the MnO2 nanowires and the MO-C2 composite, as shown in Fig. S3. Here, the MO-C2 composites were seen to display better hydrophilicity than the untreated MnO2, which is primarily because the introduction of MCQDs increased the number of functional groups on the MnO2 surface. The physical adsorption of water played a vital role in the electrochemical performance of electrode materials in aqueous electrolytes, with a greater number of contact sites between the electrode material and the electrolyte leading to better electrochemical performance. Therefore, MCQDs/MnO2 composites with better hydrophilicity showed better electrochemical performance.

In general, the perturbation of the electron density on the metal surface is related to the adsorption of the surface modifier, which directly affects the work function (WF) of the metal [39,40]. When the metal surface is modified by chemical or physical adsorption of a surface modifier having an electron-donating functional group, an interface dipole is formed with a positive electrode at the metal, which results in a significant decrease in the WF. By combining MCQDs and MnO2, the WF of MnO2 could be reduced. When the MCQDs were in contact with MnO2, electrons were transferred from the MnO2 to the MCQDs. The surface of the MnO2 nanowires formed a positive space charge region (hole-impermeable layer) which, in turn, formed an ohmic contact with the interface of the MCQDs. The resistance of the surface contact was much lower than that of the MnO2 itself, which greatly reduced the charge transfer resistance at the interface and improved the conductivity of the active material. In addition, negatively charged MCQDs could introduce electrostatic attraction towards electrolyte cations (e.g., Na+) to further promote the diffusion and utilization of ions, thus improving the electrochemical performance of transition metal oxide pseudo-capacitors.

Since the electrochemical performance of electrode materials is also affected by the porosity and specific surface area, the textural structures of the obtained samples were investigated by N2 adsorption at 77 K. The nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms and the corresponding Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) pore size distributions of the MCQDs, the MCQDs/MnO2 composites and the MnO2 nanowires are presented in Fig. 3. Based on the International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry (IUPAC) classification system, all the prepared samples exhibited linear type IV isotherms indicative of a mesoporous structure. The specific surface areas of the MCQDs, MO-C1, MO-C2, and MO-C3 composites, and the MnO2 nanowires, are seen to be 1588.8, 702.7, 1111.1, 1366.5 and 533.0 cm2 g−1, respectively. Thus, compared with the pure MnO2, the high specific surface area of the composite samples could enhance the effective transport of ions, thereby improving the electrochemical performance. In addition, the pore size distribution diagram indicates that the pore size of the MnO2 nanowires is primarily in the range of 4–20 nm, while those of the MCQDs and MCQDs/MnO2 composites are primarily in the range of 3–5 nm.

The morphologies and microstructures of the MnO2 nanowires and the MCQDs/MnO2 composites were characterized by SEM and FE-TEM. Thus, the SEM images at various magnifications ( Fig. 4) revealed the even, dense, petal-like distribution of the MCQDs/MnO2 composites, which increased the effective surface area of MnO2. Fig. 5 shows the FE-TEM images of the MCQDs, the MnO2 nanowires and the MCQDs/MnO2 composites. Here, the TEM images of the MCQDs (Fig. 5a, b) revealed the carbon nanospheres with diameters of approximately 5 nm, while the TEM images of the MnO2 nanowires (Fig. 5c, d) indicated diameters of 12 nm and lengths of over 200 nm.

In addition, the lattice plane spacing of the MnO2 nanowire surface is clearly seen to be 0.24 nm (Fig. S4), which is related to the (301) plane of the α-MnO2 nanocrystal. Finally, the FE-TEM images of the MCQDs/MnO2 composites (Fig. 5e, f) clearly indicate that the surface of the MnO2 nanowires are coated with a uniform carbon layer, with spacings of 0.24 and 0.53 nm corresponding to the (301) and (200) planes of the α-MnO2 structure. The corresponding EDS mapping and spectrum provided in Fig. S5 further verify that the MCQDs consist of the elements C, N, O, and Fe, while the MCQDs/MnO2 composites are composed of Mn, C, N, O, and Fe. This elemental distribution confirms that the MCQDs are evenly distributed on the surface of the MnO2 nanowires, which is critical for the electrochemical performance of the MnO2 supercapacitors.

In order to evaluate the capacitance performance and cyclic stability of the MnO2 nanowires and MCQDs/MnO2 composite electrodes, electrochemical measurements were performed using a three-electrode system. The cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves of the MnO2 nanowires and MCQDs/MnO2 obtained at various scan rates (5, 10, 20, 50 and 100 mV s−1) are compared in Fig. 6a-d. Here, all of the CV curves are close to rectangular in shape, and the electrode current increases with increased scan rate, which is in line with the characteristics of supercapacitors. Moreover, the shapes of the cyclic voltammetry curves are similar and symmetrical at each scan rate, thereby demonstrating the enhanced rate performance and reversible charge storage process of the electrode. Compared to the MnO2 nanowires, the MCQDs/MnO2 composite electrode carried a significantly increased current and a much greater amount of charge (indicated by the area inside the curve).

The capacitive mechanism of the MO-C2 can be determined by the relationship between the capacitance (C) and discharge time (s). The capacitance originating from surface ion adsorption/desorption remains the same independent of scan rates while the capacitance produced by the process of semi-infinite linear diffusion limitation varies with v −1/2, according to the following formula [41]:
Q = Q
v=∞ + constant (v −1/2)
where Q
v=∞ represents the capacitance contribution (i.e., the infinite scan rate capacitance), and the remaining contribution is the diffusion control capacitance, which is controlled by v −1/2. As shown in Fig. S6, for MO-C2, the y-intercept (v −1/2 = the linear fitting extrapolation of 0) yields Q
v=∞ = 80.2 mF cm−2, which means that, at 5 mV s−1, the MnO2 outer surface contributes 58.9 % of the total charge storage.

In addition, the constant current charge and discharge curves of the MnO2 nanowires and the MCQDs/MnO2 composites at various current densities (0.5, 1, 2, 3, 5, 10, 15, 20 mA cm−2) are presented in Fig. 6e-h. Here, the charge and discharge times of the four composite samples are approximately symmetrical and display certain nonlinear characteristics, thus indicating that the capacitance of MnO2 comes from two parts, namely: (i) the formation of an electric double layer and (ii) the pseudo-capacitance generated by the Faraday reaction on the surface of MnO2. Moreover, the absence of any obvious ohmic drop in the charge-discharge curve at high voltage indicates that the electrons were rapidly conducted and the internal resistance of the electrode material was small. The main reason for the decrease in specific capacity during an increase in current density is insufficient participation of the active substance in the redox reaction. In contrast, the MO-C2 sample displays a longer discharge time than the other samples. This is due to the addition of MQDs, which improved the specific surface area of the composite material. This did not only improve the adsorption/desorption of cations on the electrode surface for charge storage, but also increased the utilization rate of active materials, which was conducive to the insertion/extraction of cations in the internal phase of the electrode.

The electrochemical behavior of the electrodes was compared by using the GCD curve to evaluate the area specific capacitance, as shown in Fig. 6i. Here, the specific capacitance of the MCQDs/MnO2 composites is seen to initially increase with the addition of MCQDs until the MO-C2, after which the specific capacitance significantly decreases with the addition of more than 10 wt% MCQDs (i.e., MO-C3). This is primarily due to the obstruction of ion migration by the excessively thick carbon layer. In addition, the excess carbon inhibited the growth of crystals, which led to smaller particles and had a negative impact on the electrochemical performance of the material.

In order to investigate the effect of MCQDs coating on the cycling stability of the electrode, the electrodes were activated by 1000 pre-cycle treatments. The performance of the MnO2 nanowires was then measured over 15,000 cycles at a current density of 10 mA cm−2, and that of the MO-C2 was measured at a current density of 15 mA cm−2. The results presented in Fig. 6j indicate a 64.7 % retention of specific capacitance for the MnO2 nanowires and a 93.0 % retention for the MO-C2. However, comparison of the SEM images of the MnO2 nanowires after 15,000 cycles (Fig. S8) with those of the uncycled sample (Fig. 4a-b) indicates that the originally compact and smooth surface became rough and uneven. Moreover, the high-magnification plot in Fig. S8a-b indicates that the MnO2 nanowire structure had collapsed and partially dissolved, which is the primary reason for the serious decrease in specific capacitance. By contrast, the SEM images of the MO-C2 electrode before (Fig. 4a-b) and after cycling (Fig. S8c-d) indicate that the nanowire and petal structure remained intact. Thus, the presence of the MCQDs prevented the dissolution of MnO2, thereby improving the cycling stability of the electrode. In addition, the stable petal structure of the MO-C2 electrode vertically grown on the surface of the nickel foam provided more active sites for charge storage compared to the tightly-packed nanowire sample, so that the MO-C2 electrode displayed a higher capacitance. A proper modification strategy is essential to optimize the specific surface area, intrinsic surface reactivity and electrochemical active sites to improve electrochemical performance [24]. Normally, conventional electrode preparation methods prepare electrodes by preparing slurry with [25,42] Here, the MnO2 nanowire tightly bonded to the conductive substrate nickel foam exhibit good structure retention and maintain nanomorphology well, and the standing arrays facilitate adequate and uniform modification treatments due to their good structural dispersion and accessibility. Furthermore, the introduction of the Fe-N codoped MCQDs improves the electrical conductivity of the complex and limits the structural collapse of MnO2 during charging and discharging, resulting in better electrochemical properties.

The Coulomb efficiency of MnO2 nanowires (MO-Pure) and the various MnO2/C composites electrode (MO-C1, MO-C2 and MO-C3) was summarized in Fig. S7. Compared to the pure MnO2, the MnO2 with Fe, N-codoped MCQDs shows better coulomb efficiency. This is most likely due to the improved wettability of the MnO2 surface by Fe, N-codoped MCQDs, which reduces electrolyte wetting and some of the side reactions. As the content of Fe, N-codoped MCQDs increases, the initial Coulomb efficiency increases, but the specific capacity decreases; therefore, we choose MO-C2 as the optimal choice.

In order to further explore the effect of MCQDs coating on the kinetics of the electrochemical reaction of MnO2, the AC impedance curves (EIS) of the MnO2 nanowires and the MO-C2 were examined before and after cycling, as shown in Fig. 6k-1. The AC impedance curve of each electrode is composed of a semicircle in the high frequency region, where a smaller diameter corresponds to a smaller charge transfer resistance (Rct) at the two-phase electrode/electrolyte interface, and a diagonal line in the low frequency region, where a larger slope corresponds to a smaller ion charge insertion/extraction diffusion resistance (Rw). The results indicate that the charge transfer resistance of the material decreased to some extent after cycling, as the active site became more exposed to the electrolyte and, as the kinetics of the charge transfer process was improved, the Rct decreased with continued cycling. Moreover, a comparison of Fig. 6k and 61 reveals that the resistance of the MO-C2 electrode was less than that of the MnO2 nanowires electrode. This is because the functional groups on the surface of the MCQDs increased the wettability of the electrode material by the electrolyte and reduced the charge transfer resistance. According to the mass-transfer control in the low frequency region and the charger-transfer control in the high frequency region, it means Fe, N-codoped MCQDs enhance the electronic conductivity of transition metal oxides to some extent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical performance of MO-C2//MO-C2 symmetrical supercapacitors
ce:section.ce:para: To further evaluate the practical application of the MO-C2 electrode, a symmetrical supercapacitor device was assembled and tested in PVA/Na2SO4 electrolyte, as shown in the schematic illustration in Fig. S9. To determine a good working potential, the CV (at 50 mV s−1) and the GCD of the MO-C2//MO-C2 symmetrical supercapacitor were measured under various potential windows (i.e., 0–1 V, 0–1.4 V, 0–1.6 V and 0–1.8 V), as shown in Fig. 7a. Thus, while no ideal polarization behavior was observed in the voltage range of 0–1.6 V, an obvious hump appears in the GCD curve in the range of 0–1.8 V; hence, 0–1.6 V was selected as the appropriate operating voltage window. The CV curves of the symmetric device at various sweep speeds were then obtained, as shown in Fig. 7b. Thus, as the scan rate was increased from 10 mV s−1 to 200 mV s−1, the CV curve maintained a regular rectangular shape with no obvious changes, which indicates that the MO-C2 electrode had a fast redox rate and low internal resistance indicative of good capacitive behavior. In addition, the good flexibility and stability of the solid-state symmetric supercapacitor device are demonstrated by the CV curves obtained at various bending angles shown in Fig. S10.

The GCD curves of the symmetrical supercapacitors at various current densities are presented in Fig. 7c, while the curves obtained under various voltage windows are given in Fig. S11. The approximately linear and symmetrical nature of the GCD curve in Fig. 7c is characteristic of the electrochemical behavior of an ideal capacitor. In addition, the MO-C2//MO-C2 device was subjected to 10,000 charge/discharge cycles at a constant current of 2 mA cm−2 (Fig. 7d) to indicate a capacitance retention of 85.6 %. After that, the device was allowed to stand for 1 h, before another 5,000 cycles at a current density of 3 mA cm−2 and the device finally exhibited a capacitance retention of 73.7 %.

The mechanism behind the excellent cycle performance was investigated via electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). Thus, in Fig. 7e, the Rs values of the MO-C2 symmetric supercapacitor before and after 15,000 cycles are seen to be 1.8 and 2.1 Ω, respectively, along with only a slight increase in the Rct after the cycles, thus demonstrating the good ionic conductivity of the electrode/electrolyte interface. In addition, an increased slope in the low frequency region indicates an increase in the ion diffusion rate after activation. This is the rationale for pre-cycling before the test and also explains the oft-reported increase in the specific capacitance of devices during the first hundreds to thousands of cycles in the literature. The Ragone diagram in Fig. 7f clearly demonstrates the superior energy and power density of the symmetric MO-C2//MO-C2 supercapacitor compared with those reported in the literature [43–48]. The maximum area energy density of the MO-C2//MO-C2 symmetric supercapacitor device was 11.16 mW h cm−2 at a power density of 311.6 mW cm−2, and the maximum power density was 4.8 W cm−2 at an energy density of 2.3 mW h cm−2.

A simple method for the preparation of a binder-free metal carbon quantum dots/MnO2 nanowire (MCQDs/MnO2) composite flexible solid supercapacitor was described herein. Most of the conventional techniques for preparing quantum dots suffer from serious drawbacks, such as complicated process, toxic starting materials, and high cost, which largely limit their practical applications. We developed a facile method for the preparation of N, Fe co-doped MCQDs/MnO2 composites. In this process, nitrogen doping could effectively induce a large number of defects within the carbon microstructure, increasing the available specific surface area for more ion accommodation. In addition, we employed an in situ formation of metalorganics for iron doping, which improved the dispersibility of metal ions. The uniformly dispersed iron metal ions not only improved the electrical conductivity of the composites, but also participated in redox reactions in the form of metal oxides, which helped to improve specific capacity and rate capability. Finally, size effects could also have a positive effect, with nanoscale MCQDs providing more active sites and contributing to improving the specific capacity. The experimental results demonstrated the good hydrophilicity and pore size distribution of the carbon-coated MnO2 nanowires, which led to improved wettability of the electrode material by the electrolyte, decreased ion diffusion resistance, enhanced utilization rate of the electrode material, and increased specific capacitance. At the same time, the coated carbon was shown to limit the collapse and shedding of the MnO2 structure during the charging and discharging process, and to improve the cycling stability of the material. The maximum energy density of the symmetric supercapacitor prepared with MnO2/MCQDs composite was 11.16 mWh cm−2 at a power density of 311.6 mW cm−2, and the maximum power density was 4.8 W cm−2 at an energy density was 2.3 mWh cm−2. These excellent electrochemical performances and mechanical flexibility demonstrate a facial method for the preparation of composite materials as electrode for flexible energy storage devices.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank Wenner-Gren Foundations (UPD2021-0123) for financial support.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jallcom.2023.171021.

ce:section.ce:para:
